data.MechanismNote: The stratum variable affects outcome (easy vs hard conditions) but does NOT affect treatment assignment — each treatment receives exactly 50% of its cases in each stratum. Because treatment assignment is independent of stratum, no confounding can occur and the pooled rate must agree with every per-stratum comparison.
data.Name: balanced-synthetic-stratum

data.MechanismNote: Case difficulty affects both which treatment patients receive (A is over-allocated to hard cases: 60% vs 40%) and the outcome (hard cases have lower success rates for both treatments). This IS confounding — but the confounding is not strong enough to flip the pooled winner. A wins in every stratum and also wins pooled. The allocation compresses A's pooled advantage (from equal-weight 10pp down to actual 6pp) but does not erase it. This is type-C distortion: real confounding, partial distortion, no reversal.
data.Name: compressed-synthetic-stratum

data.MechanismNote: Hospital baseline UTI incidence affects both whether a patient received prophylaxis (high-incidence hospitals were more likely to forgo it, concentrating controls there) and the outcome (high-incidence hospitals have higher UTI rates regardless of treatment). Most prophylaxis patients are in low-incidence hospitals (1113/1279=87%), which depresses the aggregate prophylaxis UTI rate below the aggregate control rate — inverting the within-stratum finding.
data.Name: reintjes-2000-hospital-incidence

data.MechanismNote: Victim race affects both defendant race (cross-race homicides are rare — most black defendants killed black victims, most white defendants killed white victims) and sentencing outcome (white-victim cases receive the death penalty ~5× more often: ~17.7% vs ~3.3%). This is classic confounding. Black defendants are disproportionately concentrated in the black-victim stratum (low sentence rate), pulling their aggregate death penalty rate below white defendants' rate — even though black defendants are MORE likely to be sentenced to death within each victim-race stratum.
data.Name: radelet-1981-victim-race

data.MechanismNote: The season year affects both the player's at-bat count (treatment assignment proxy — Justice's heaviest season was 1995 when his average was lowest; Jeter's heaviest were 1996-97 when his average was higher) and the outcome (players hit differently in different years). Justice front-loaded his ABs into his worst year (411 of 1046 = 39% in 1995 at .253); Jeter back-loaded into his stronger years (1236 of 1284 = 96% in 1996-97 at .300-.314). This differential allocation of AB-weight across years flips the combined average despite Justice winning every year individually.
data.Name: jeter-justice-1997-season

data.MechanismNote: Age at baseline confounds both smoking status and 20-year mortality. Smoking affects age distribution in the cohort (survivorship bias: people who smoked heavily and were older had already died before the 1972-74 baseline survey, so the surviving smokers skew younger). Age strongly determines 20-year mortality regardless of smoking. The result: smokers are over-represented in low-mortality age strata (18-44), pulling the pooled smoker mortality rate below the true within-stratum risk. This is the textbook epidemiological confounding by age pattern, often called the "healthy smoker" fallacy in this particular dataset.
data.Name: appleton-1996-age

data.MechanismNote: Age group is a powerful confounder. It affects vaccine uptake (older people were prioritised and had much higher vaccination rates in the UK's 2021 rollout — 23% of vaccinated are 50+ vs only 2.3% of unvaccinated) and strongly determines COVID-19 mortality (50+ CFR is ~40× higher than under-50 CFR in this dataset regardless of vaccination). The vaccinated cohort is therefore loaded with high-risk patients, inflating the pooled vaccinated death rate to 0.41% vs 0.17% for unvaccinated — reversing the within-stratum relationship where vaccines reduce death rates by 3× to 4× in every age group.
data.Name: phe-covid-2021-age

data.MechanismNote: Patient risk class affects both hospital assignment (high-volume centers receive referrals of sicker patients — 70% of A caseload is high-risk vs only 20% at B) and surgical outcome (high-risk patients have dramatically higher operative mortality regardless of hospital volume). High-volume hospitals win in every risk stratum (15pp advantage in high-risk, 2pp in low-risk) and also win pooled — but their pooled survival advantage is compressed from 7.9pp (equal-weight stratum average) down to 2.8pp (actual pooled) because they carry so many harder cases. IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion≈0.05 — the geometry of type-C distortion: real confounding, partial distortion, no reversal.
data.Name: hannan-1994-patient-risk

data.MechanismNote: Passenger sex affects both cabin class (1st class had a higher fraction of women — 45% vs 26% in 3rd class, reflecting social travel patterns of the era) and survival outcome ('women and children first' evacuation protocol gave women dramatically higher survival rates regardless of class). The over-representation of women in 1st class inflates the pooled 1st-class survival advantage beyond the per-stratum (per-sex) average — the pooled gap (37.4pp) exceeds the equal-weight average (33.4pp). AllocationDistortion>0, IsSignFlip=FALSE → type-C.
data.Name: titanic-1912-sex

data.MechanismNote: Birth weight category is a mediator, not a classical confounder — it lies on the causal pathway from maternal smoking to infant mortality (smoking causes low birth weight, which causes elevated mortality). Conditioning on birth weight (stratifying) opens a collider path: among LBW infants, smoker babies are 'selected in' via a milder mechanism (growth restriction) while non-smoker LBW babies are more likely to have serious congenital causes. This creates the paradox: smokers appear to have better LBW survival not because smoking is protective, but because the comparison groups differ in unobserved severity. IsSignFlip=TRUE, RI=0.5 → type-B (partial reversal: smokers win LBW stratum, non-smokers win NBW, non-smokers win pooled).
data.Name: bwp-weight-class

data.MechanismNote: Patient sex affects both tumor thickness at presentation (women self-examine and seek medical attention earlier — 70% of female melanomas are thin vs 40% of male melanomas) and survival outcome (beyond the thickness effect, female sex is an independent prognostic factor for melanoma). Women's concentration in the high-survival thin-tumor stratum inflates their pooled survival advantage from 7.9pp (equal-weight per-stratum average) to 18pp (actual pooled). IsSignFlip=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0.094 → type-C inflation.
data.Name: melanoma-sex

data.MechanismNote: Smoking status affects CHD outcome (smokers have higher baseline CHD risk) but does NOT materially affect coffee consumption (equal allocation across strata in this study design). Because treatment assignment is effectively independent of the confounder, no allocation imbalance occurs and the pooled estimate agrees with the per-stratum estimates. AllocationDistortion≈0 → type-D: no Simpson distortion. This study is the clean null case — confounders that do not create allocation imbalance do not distort the pooled result.
data.Name: coffee-smoking

data.MechanismNote: State school spending confounds the participation-rate / SAT-score comparison. High-spending states tend to have high participation (more students take the SAT, including weaker students), and high spending is independently associated with better educational outcomes. Within each spending stratum, high-participation states do better (or tie), because controlling for spending removes the quality signal. But in high-spending states, the small self-selected pool of test-takers from low-participation states outscores the broad pool from high-participation states — reversing the low-spending stratum winner. IsSignFlip=TRUE, RI=0.5 → type-B partial reversal.
data.Name: sat-school-spending

TreatmentRankings.Description: Table: TreatmentRankings — one row per treatment-pair comparison within a study. Derives the pooled winner, the per-stratum winner, and the IsReversal boolean. The paradox emerges here as a derived fact: IsReversal is true when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree.

schema.name: TreatmentRankingId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. kidney-1986-A-vs-B).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: Study
schema.Description: FK to the parent Study.

schema.name: TreatmentA
schema.Description: Label for the first treatment in the comparison (matches Treatments.TreatmentLabel).

schema.name: TreatmentB
schema.Description: Label for the second treatment in the comparison.

schema.name: TotalCasesA
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentA across all strata in this study.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesA
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentA across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateA
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentA: TotalSuccessesA / TotalCasesA.

schema.name: TotalCasesB
schema.Description: Total cases for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: TotalSuccessesB
schema.Description: Total successes for TreatmentB across all strata.

schema.name: PooledRateB
schema.Description: Pooled success rate for TreatmentB: TotalSuccessesB / TotalCasesB.

schema.name: PooledWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins when strata are ignored: the one with the higher pooled rate.

schema.name: StratumCount
schema.Description: Number of strata in this study. Counted from StratumSummaries filtered to TreatmentA rows (one row per stratum).

schema.name: StrataWonByA
schema.Description: Number of strata where A wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentA. These are the one-per-stratum sentinel rows.

schema.name: StrataWonByB
schema.Description: Number of strata where B wins: count StratumSummaries rows whose TreatmentLabel=TreatmentA AND StratumWinner=TreatmentB.

schema.name: PerStratumWinner
schema.Description: The treatment that wins in every stratum — 'A' if StrataWonByA = StratumCount, 'B' if StrataWonByB = StratumCount, else 'none' (no unanimous per-stratum winner).

schema.name: IsReversal
schema.Description: TRUE when the pooled winner and the per-stratum winner disagree — i.e. Simpson's Paradox is present. FALSE otherwise. This is the paradox as a derived fact, not a modeled entity.

schema.name: ConfoundersInStudy
schema.Description: Count of StratumVariables in this study whose IsConfounder = TRUE. When > 0, the paradox has a causal explanation.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The model witnesses its own explanatory completeness — or its limits.

schema.name: IsParadoxExplained_v2
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal_v2 is present AND at least one confirmed confounder exists in the study. The v2 counterpart to IsParadoxExplained — captures berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996, which have confirmed confounders but do not satisfy the unanimity criterion. Together with IsParadoxExplained, this field witnesses exactly which studies the old definition left unexplained despite having a causal account.

schema.name: PooledGap
schema.Description: Absolute difference between the two pooled rates: |PooledRateA - PooledRateB|. The size of the aggregate misleading signal.

schema.name: StrataWonByLoser
schema.Description: Number of strata won by the pooled loser — the counter-signal. For full reversals this equals StratumCount; for partial paradoxes it is between 0 and StratumCount.

schema.name: ParadoxStrength
schema.Description: Scalar severity of the paradox: PooledGap × (StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount). Zero when no strata go against the pooled winner. Positive for partial paradoxes. Maximum when every stratum contradicts the pooled result.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsA
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentA reconstructed as a weighted average of stratum rates: SUM(WeightedStratumRate) across all stratum rows for TreatmentA. Must match PooledRateA — this witnesses the mechanism equation: the pooled rate IS a weighted average of stratum rates, weighted by StratumFraction.

schema.name: PooledRateFromWeightsB
schema.Description: Pooled rate for TreatmentB reconstructed as a weighted average. Must match PooledRateB. If it does, the mechanism equation is verified: reversal is purely a consequence of differential weighting.

schema.name: ReversalIntensity
schema.Description: StrataWonByLoser / StratumCount: the fraction of strata that go against the pooled winner. Zero for no paradox, 1.0 for full reversal, between 0 and 1 for partial. This is the allocation-side measure of how deeply the confounding has penetrated the stratified view.

schema.name: ThresholdMargin
schema.Description: ReversalIntensity minus 0.5: positive when more than half the strata go against the pooled winner (reversal is robust), zero at the tipping point (exactly half), negative when fewer than half oppose the pooled winner (reversal is fragile or absent). A study with ThresholdMargin > 0 is robustly paradoxical; one with ThresholdMargin < 0 has a weak or absent paradox.

schema.name: SignedPooledGap
schema.Description: Signed difference between pooled rates: PooledRateA minus PooledRateB. Positive = A wins pooled; negative = B wins pooled. Unlike PooledGap (which is absolute), this preserves direction — essential for detecting sign flips between the equal-weight and actual pooled signals.

schema.name: WeightedStratumGapSum
schema.Description: Sum of WeightedStratumGap (= StratumGap × StratumFraction) across all StratumSummaries rows where TreatmentLabel = TreatmentA for this study. This is the equal-weight pooled gap — the gap the pooled analysis would produce if allocation were perfectly balanced across strata. When this has the opposite sign to SignedPooledGap, the allocation has not merely compressed the signal: it has flipped it entirely.

schema.name: IsSignFlip
schema.Description: TRUE when WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs — the allocation has reversed the direction of the pooled signal relative to what equal-weight strata would show. This is the continuous generalisation of IsReversal: it does not require every stratum to agree, only that the equal-weight estimate and the actual pooled estimate point in opposite directions. Berkeley 1973 satisfies IsSignFlip=TRUE despite IsReversal=FALSE.

schema.name: AllocationDistortion
schema.Description: The magnitude of how far the allocation has bent the pooled signal: |WeightedStratumGapSum − SignedPooledGap|. Zero means the allocation is neutral — the pooled analysis faithfully represents the equal-weight stratum evidence. A large AllocationDistortion means the allocation is doing most of the work: the pooled number is mostly noise from how cases were assigned, not signal about which treatment is better. This measure does NOT require a sign flip to be nonzero — it captures any allocation-induced distortion, not just reversals.

schema.name: DistortionType
schema.Description: Geometric classification of this study's allocation-induced distortion. Four exhaustive types: A = full sign-flip with unanimous per-stratum reversal (IsSignFlip=TRUE AND ReversalIntensity=1.0); B = sign-flip but not unanimous (IsSignFlip=TRUE AND ReversalIntensity<1.0); C = distortion without sign flip (IsSignFlip=FALSE AND AllocationDistortion>0.01 — the allocation has compressed or inflated the pooled margin but not reversed it); D = effectively neutral (AllocationDistortion≤0.01 — pooled analysis is trustworthy with respect to this stratification). The taxonomy is derived purely from the geometric coordinates; it does not depend on causal attribution.

schema.name: PolicyImplication
schema.Description: The researcher action implied by the geometric classification. Derived from DistortionType: type A (full reversal) → 'stratify-immediately' — the pooled conclusion is directionally wrong; do not act on it without stratifying. Type B (partial sign-flip) → 'investigate-confounder' — the equal-weight signal opposes the pooled signal, but the per-stratum picture is mixed; the causal mechanism needs examination before trusting either estimate. Type C (compression without flip) → 'check-allocation-bias' — the pooled winner is probably correct but the effect size is distorted; report adjusted or standardized margins rather than the raw pooled gap. Type D (neutral) → 'pooled-analysis-trustworthy' — allocation is not materially distorting the signal; the pooled conclusion is safe with respect to this stratification.

schema.name: IsReversal_v2
schema.Description: The sign-flip definition of reversal: TRUE when the equal-weight pooled signal (WeightedStratumGapSum) and the actual pooled signal (SignedPooledGap) point in opposite directions. Strictly generalizes the unanimity criterion — every Type-A study satisfies both IsReversal and IsReversal_v2, but Berkeley-1973 and Appleton-1996 satisfy IsReversal_v2 without satisfying IsReversal. The unanimity criterion (IsReversal) is now understood as the Type-A subtype of this broader definition.

schema.name: DefinitionDelta
schema.Description: TRUE when IsReversal_v2 and IsReversal disagree — i.e. when the sign-flip definition fires but the unanimity criterion does not. These are the studies the old definition misclassified as non-reversals. In the current dataset: berkeley-1973 and appleton-1996. A non-empty DefinitionDelta set is the empirical proof that the unanimity criterion is a strict subset of the sign-flip criterion.

schema.name: StrictReversalSubtype
schema.Description: Classifies each study's relationship to the two reversal definitions. 'strict' = unanimity reversal (IsReversal=TRUE, DistortionType=A — the old definition's true positives). 'extended' = sign-flip only, not unanimous (IsReversal_v2=TRUE, IsReversal=FALSE — the studies the old definition missed). 'none' = no reversal under either definition.

schema.name: CorrectedGap
schema.Description: The allocation-corrected treatment gap: WeightedStratumGapSum, i.e. what SignedPooledGap would be if allocation were equal across strata. Positive means A is favoured in the corrected world; negative means B is favoured.

schema.name: CorrectedWinner
schema.Description: Which treatment wins once allocation bias is removed. Derived from CorrectedGap: positive → TreatmentA; negative → TreatmentB; zero → tie.

schema.name: CorrectedVsPooledAgreement
schema.Description: TRUE when the allocation-corrected winner matches the pooled winner. FALSE when removing allocation bias would change which treatment appears to win — the machine-readable definition of a reversal recovery.

schema.name: CorrectedPolicyImplication
schema.Description: The allocation-aware researcher action: what to do when the corrected verdict is available. Derived from DistortionType and CorrectedWinner together, making the instrument self-consistent with Reversal Recovery (loop-27). Type A (full sign-flip, unanimous per-stratum reversal): CorrectedWinner is the true signal — 'use-corrected-winner'. The pooled analysis was directionally wrong; act on CorrectedWinner, not PooledWinner. Type B (partial sign-flip, non-unanimous): CorrectedWinner points against the pooled signal but strata disagree among themselves — 'use-corrected-winner-with-caution'. Trust the corrected direction but acknowledge residual uncertainty. Type C (compression, no sign flip): PooledWinner is directionally correct; the distortion is in the magnitude, not the direction — 'check-allocation-bias'. Same as PolicyImplication. Type D (neutral): allocation is not materially distorting the signal — 'pooled-analysis-trustworthy'. Same as PolicyImplication. For Types C and D, CorrectedPolicyImplication and PolicyImplication always agree; for Types A and B, CorrectedPolicyImplication supersedes PolicyImplication with the allocation-corrected verdict.

InvariantChecks.Description: Table: InvariantChecks — one row per algebraic invariant that must hold across the full validation set. PassCount and FailCount are populated by 05b-customize-data.sql from live queries against vw_treatment_rankings. Any FailCount > 0 is a build-breaking bug.

schema.name: InvariantCheckId
schema.Description: Unique slug for this invariant (e.g. inv-balanced-alloc).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: AlgebraicStatement
schema.Description: The mathematical assertion in compact symbolic form.

schema.name: NaturalLanguage
schema.Description: Plain-English statement of what property must hold and why it is an invariant of the model.

schema.name: SourceTable
schema.Description: The DAG table whose rows are checked (TreatmentRankings or StratumSummaries).

schema.name: FilterExpression
schema.Description: Condition selecting the universe of rows this invariant applies to. Empty means all rows.

schema.name: AssertionExpression
schema.Description: The per-row condition that must be TRUE for every row in scope.

schema.name: PassCount
schema.Description: Rows where the invariant holds. Populated by 05b-customize-data.sql from a live query against vw_treatment_rankings.

schema.name: FailCount
schema.Description: Rows where the invariant is violated. Any FailCount > 0 is a build-breaking bug.

schema.name: UniverseCount
schema.Description: Total rows in scope (PassCount + FailCount).

schema.name: IsGreen
schema.Description: TRUE when FailCount = 0.

schema.name: StatusLabel
schema.Description: PASS or FAIL(n) summary for display.

schema.name: Severity
schema.Description: critical | warning — critical means any FailCount > 0 is a build-breaking bug.

data.NaturalLanguage: Every TreatmentRanking row must have a non-null DistortionType. A blank classification means the DAG has a gap.
data.AssertionExpression: DistortionType IS NOT NULL AND DistortionType <> ''

data.NaturalLanguage: Types A and B are defined as sign-flip reversals. A study classified A or B where IsSignFlip is FALSE violates the type definition.
Types C and D are non-reversal types. A C or D study where IsSignFlip is TRUE is a classification contradiction.
When pooled and equal-weight signals point in opposite directions (IsSignFlip), the corrected winner must differ from the pooled winner. This is the definition of a sign flip.
Type-D studies have negligible allocation distortion so corrected and pooled winners must agree. Disagreement means the type classification is wrong.
Type D is defined as allocation-free (distortion below the 1pp threshold). Any Type-D row with AllocationDistortion ≥ 0.01 violates the classification invariant.
CorrectedGap is defined as WeightedStratumGapSum. Their signs must always agree — a mismatch would mean the corrected winner formula reads the wrong field.

__meta__.Description: Table: __meta__ — project-level metadata for the Simpson's Paradox domain. One row per key. Transparent to transpilers.

schema.name: MetaKey
schema.Description: Unique key identifying this metadata entry.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors MetaKey.

schema.name: ValueType
schema.Description: Type of value stored: string | object | array

schema.name: StringValue
schema.Description: Value when ValueType = string.

schema.name: JsonValue
schema.Description: JSON-encoded value when ValueType = object or array.

data.StringValue: Domain mirror of Simpson's Paradox. Entities: Studies, Treatments, Strata, CaseCells. CellSuccessRate is the first derived fact. Pooled rates, per-stratum rates, and the reversal fall out as higher-order derived nodes in subsequent loops.
A digital mirror of Simpson's Paradox — the paradox falls out of the DAG as a derived fact, never modeled directly.

data.JsonValue: ["Teaching Simpson's Paradox with real data", "Classifying studies by distortion geometry", "Generating researcher action (PolicyImplication) from allocation bias", "Prospective literature screening for confounded pooled conclusions"]

data.StringValue: The instrument witnessed its first full type-A reversal from real published data (reintjes-2000 and radelet-1981) in loop-20b. Berkeley (loop-08) introduced the partial reversal and the sign-flip definition replaced the unanimity criterion in loop-17. The four-type taxonomy (A/B/C/D) was completed in loop-21.

Methodology.Description: Table: Methodology — the first-principles process used to build this domain model. Each row documents one methodological axiom or step. Together these rows let a reader (or an LLM) reconstruct why the model is shaped the way it is, and re-apply the same process to a new domain.

schema.name: MethodologyId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. method-01-cmcc, method-02-dag-first).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors MethodologyId.

schema.name: Phase
schema.Description: Broad phase: axiom | decomposition | derivation | hydration | validation | taxonomy | conclusion

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Short title of the methodological principle or step.

schema.name: Statement
schema.Description: One-to-three sentence statement of the principle, precise enough to be applied or falsified.

schema.name: WhyItMatters
schema.Description: Why this step or principle is load-bearing for this model — what breaks if you skip it.

schema.name: IllustratedByLoop
schema.Description: LoopId(s) where this principle was concretely demonstrated (comma-separated).

data.Title: CMCC: any sufficiently crisp conceptual world decomposes into a DAG of S/D/L/A/F primitives
data.Statement: The Conceptual Model Conjecture (CMCC) holds that every domain can be decomposed into Schema, Data, Lookups, Aggregations, and Functions arranged as a directed acyclic graph. Each node is locally simple; complexity emerges from composition, not from any single node. This is the working assumption for the entire model, not a hypothesis to relitigate each turn.
data.WhyItMatters: If CMCC holds, the paradox can be derived rather than modeled. The model does not need a 'ReversalDetection' entity — it just needs the right leaf data and the right DAG edges. The paradox falls out as a consequence.

data.Title: Model the domain entities first; derived facts emerge from the DAG
data.Statement: Start with the empirical leaf data: Cases and Successes in each (Study, Stratum, Treatment) cell. Never model the phenomenon of interest (the paradox) directly. The paradox is a derived fact — it emerges when you ask the right questions of a correctly-shaped DAG.
data.WhyItMatters: Modeling the paradox directly (e.g. a 'ReversalDetection' table) would conflate the phenomenon with the domain. The domain is Studies, Treatments, Strata, and CaseCells. The paradox is a read on that domain.

data.Title: Build the model incrementally via Leopold Loops — one new concept per loop
data.Statement: Each loop introduces exactly one new first-class concept and asks the natural-language question that concept answers. The loop ends when the new derived fact is witnessed in the database. Loop N's NextSuggestion seeds Loop N+1.
data.WhyItMatters: Introducing multiple concepts per loop makes it hard to witness whether each concept earns its place. One concept per loop forces the question: 'what new thing can I now ask that I couldn't before?'

data.Title: Every derived fact must be witnessed from the database, not claimed from the rulebook
data.Statement: A field is not 'done' when it is added to the rulebook. It is done when a Postgres query returns the expected value for the seeded data. MockDataNote in each Loop row states what value is expected and from which study. If the DB returns a different value, the formula or the seed data is wrong.
data.WhyItMatters: Without witnessing, silent fallbacks and mistranslations accumulate invisibly. The rulebook says CellSuccessRate = Successes/Cases; the DB must actually return 0.931 for kidney small-A.

data.Title: Introduce synthetic control studies to test structural impossibility
data.Statement: For every interesting derived boolean (IsReversal, IsSignFlip), add at least one synthetic study where the boolean MUST be false by construction. Witnessing the false case in the DB proves the derivation is not vacuously true.
data.WhyItMatters: Without a structural-impossibility witness, a bug in the formula that always returns TRUE would pass all the positive tests. The balanced-synthetic and kidney-balanced studies exist solely to witness IsReversal=FALSE and AllocationDistortion=0.

data.Title: Real study data lives in the rulebook JSON — the SSoT is unified
data.Statement: The rulebook JSON is the single source of truth for both schema and instance data. Real published study counts (Successes, Cases per cell) live directly in each table's data array alongside the synthetic studies. Provenance for each real study is recorded in data/raw/<study-id>/provenance.md. The view chain computes every derived field from the rulebook without any separate hydration step.
data.WhyItMatters: Keeping schema and instance data together in one document makes the instrument self-contained and auditable. The rulebook JSON can be shared, forked, or loaded without a separate SQL hydration step. The schema still defines derivations; the data rows supply the raw inputs.

data.Title: Replace binary reversal criterion with continuous geometry: IsSignFlip + AllocationDistortion
data.Statement: The original IsReversal (unanimous per-stratum winner disagrees with pooled winner) is a binary that excludes partial reversals. The sign-flip definition (WeightedStratumGapSum and SignedPooledGap have opposite signs) captures the full continuum. AllocationDistortion = |WSGSUM − SignedPooledGap| is the scalar severity regardless of direction.
data.WhyItMatters: Berkeley 1973 fails the unanimity criterion (dept-C gives male a marginal win) but IS a Simpson-type paradox under the continuous definition. Excluding Berkeley from the analysis would be a category error.

data.Title: Classify studies into four types (A/B/C/D) by distortion geometry, independent of causal label
data.Statement: Type A = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity=1.0 (full unanimous reversal). Type B = IsSignFlip AND ReversalIntensity<1.0 (partial sign-flip). Type C = NOT IsSignFlip AND AllocationDistortion>threshold (compression without flip). Type D = AllocationDistortion≈0 (neutral). The geometry is computed from allocation and rates alone — no causal story is required.
data.WhyItMatters: The taxonomy makes PolicyImplication derivable. A researcher reading DistortionType=A knows to stratify immediately without needing to know what caused the allocation imbalance.

data.Title: The model witnesses its own epistemic coverage via ModelSummary
data.Statement: ModelSummary is a single-row table that aggregates ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, and AvgAllocationDistortion across all studies. It is the model's self-portrait — a derived fact about the model's own completeness.
data.WhyItMatters: Without ModelSummary, you can query any individual study but cannot ask 'how many studies does this instrument explain?' The self-portrait is the epistemic coverage claim.

data.Title: Policy implication is derived from geometry, not asserted from domain knowledge
data.Statement: PolicyImplication is a calculated field derived from DistortionType. No domain-specific knowledge about medicine, law, or education is encoded. The instrument tells a researcher what to do next with the pooled conclusion (stratify, investigate, check bias, or trust) purely from the allocation geometry.
data.WhyItMatters: Domain-neutral policy derivation means the instrument generalizes across clinical, judicial, educational, and other settings without re-tuning. The same formula works for kidney stones, Berkeley admissions, and antibiotic prophylaxis.

Conclusions.Description: Table: Conclusions — the substantive epistemic findings that this model has produced or is designed to produce. Each row is one conclusion: a claim about the domain, about the instrument, or about the methodology that is either already witnessed or is the target of a planned loop.

schema.name: ConclusionId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. conc-01-paradox-is-derived).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors ConclusionId.

schema.name: Category
schema.Description: domain | instrument | methodology | open-question

schema.name: Status
schema.Description: witnessed | planned | open

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: One-line statement of the conclusion.

schema.name: Evidence
schema.Description: The specific DB query result, loop outcome, or argument that establishes or will establish this conclusion.

schema.name: WitnessedInLoop
schema.Description: LoopId where this conclusion was first witnessed (if status=witnessed).

schema.name: TargetLoop
schema.Description: LoopId that is designed to witness this conclusion (if status=planned).

data.Title: Simpson's Paradox is a derived fact from the DAG, not a modeled entity
data.Evidence: IsReversal (and later IsSignFlip) falls out as a calculated field on TreatmentRankings, derived from aggregated CaseCell counts. No 'ReversalDetection' entity was ever needed.

data.Title: The reversal in kidney-1986 is entirely an allocation artifact — same rates with balanced allocation produce no reversal
data.Evidence: kidney-balanced uses identical stratum success rates to kidney-1986 but with equal allocation (175 cases per cell). IsReversal=FALSE, AllocationDistortion=0. The paradox is algebraically isolated as a property of allocation, not of treatment efficacy.

data.Title: The unanimity criterion (IsReversal) incorrectly excludes Berkeley 1973 despite its being a Simpson-type paradox
data.Evidence: Berkeley: WSGSUM=−0.0642, SignedPooledGap=+0.1292, IsSignFlip=TRUE, AllocationDistortion=0.1934 — greater distortion than kidney-1986. But IsReversal=FALSE because dept-C marginally favors male. The unanimity criterion is a strict subtype, not the primary definition.

data.Title: Allocation can compress the pooled signal without flipping it (type-C): a class invisible to IsReversal and IsSignFlip but visible to AllocationDistortion
data.Evidence: compressed-synthetic: A wins every stratum and wins pooled, but the pooled margin is 6pp rather than the equal-weight 10pp. AllocationDistortion=0.0400, IsSignFlip=FALSE. The pooled winner is correct; the effect size is wrong by 4pp.

data.Title: Two real published studies (reintjes-2000, radelet-1981) hydrate as type-A full reversals without schema changes
data.Evidence: Both studies entered via 05b-customize-data.sql. Witnessed: reintjes AllocationDistortion=0.0516, DistortionType=A; radelet AllocationDistortion=0.0684, DistortionType=A. No DAG or schema change was needed — the hydration contract generalizes.

data.Title: PolicyImplication is domain-neutral: the same formula produces researcher actions across medicine, law, and education
data.Evidence: All five current studies (kidney-1986, berkeley-1973, compressed-synthetic, kidney-balanced, balanced-synthetic) plus two real studies produce unambiguous PolicyImplication values from DistortionType alone. No domain-specific knowledge is encoded in the formula.

data.Title: ModelSummary witnesses the instrument's own epistemic coverage as a first-class derived fact
data.Evidence: ModelSummary aggregates ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeA/B/C/D counts, and AvgAllocationDistortion across all studies. The model describes its own completeness in a single row.

data.Title: Does the published literature contain real type-C studies (compression without sign flip)?
data.Evidence: Witnessed in loop-24: hannan-1994 (CABG surgery), titanic-1912, melanoma-altman-1991, and coffee-tverdal-2020 all hydrate as DistortionType=C. AllocationDistortion>0.01, IsSignFlip=FALSE — the correct pooled direction but compressed magnitude. The type-C cell is not a synthetic pathology.

data.Title: Does AllocationDistortion measure causal confounding, or purely arithmetic distortion?
data.Evidence: Berkeley has the highest AllocationDistortion (0.1934) but CausalRole=contested (mediator vs confounder). The open question is whether distortion geometry and causal structure are correlated, and if so, how strongly.

data.Title: The full instrument can be specified precisely enough for any researcher to apply it to a new study
data.Evidence: Witnessed in loop-26: InstrumentSpec table enumerates 5 input fields, 10 derived coordinates, 2 classification rules, and 3 adapter-requirement rows. Machine-verifiable against all 16 studies in the validation set.

data.Title: The model can produce the allocation-corrected winner directly from existing DAG fields — no new data required
data.Evidence: CorrectedGap = WeightedStratumGapSum (already derived in loop-12/17). CorrectedWinner is a sign-read on CorrectedGap. CorrectedVsPooledAgreement is TRUE for Type-D studies and FALSE for Type-A/B studies. For kidney-1986: CorrectedGap=+0.0537, CorrectedWinner=A, CorrectedVsPooledAgreement=FALSE (pooled said B; corrected says A). The reversal recovery is free — it costs zero new data.

UIScreens.Description: Table: UIScreens — the canonical screens that comprise the Simpson's Paradox Explorer UI. Each row defines one screen: its purpose, its primary data source (which rulebook view it reads), and the user action it supports. A developer can reconstruct the full UI from this table without any prior knowledge of the application.

schema.name: ScreenId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. screen-01-study-list).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors ScreenId.

schema.name: Title
schema.Description: Human-readable screen title shown in the UI.

schema.name: Route
schema.Description: URL path pattern (e.g. /, /study/:studyId, /compare).

schema.name: PrimaryEntity
schema.Description: The main rulebook entity this screen reads from (the vw_* view name without prefix).

schema.name: Purpose
schema.Description: One sentence: what a user can learn or do on this screen.

schema.name: KeyFields
schema.Description: Comma-separated list of the most important fields displayed on this screen.

schema.name: UserAction
schema.Description: Primary user action available on this screen (e.g. select a study, drill into a stratum).

data.Title: Study List
data.Purpose: Show all studies with their DistortionType badge and AllocationDistortion magnitude so a user can immediately see which studies exhibit the paradox and how severely.
data.KeyFields: Title, DistortionType (from TreatmentRankings), AllocationDistortion, PolicyImplication, IsSignFlip, TotalCases
data.UserAction: Click a study row to navigate to the Study Detail screen.

data.Title: Study Detail
data.Purpose: Show one study's complete geometry: pooled rates, per-stratum rates, allocation fractions, AllocationDistortion, DistortionType, and PolicyImplication — the full causal picture for this study.
data.KeyFields: PooledRateA, PooledRateB, PooledWinner, PerStratumWinner, IsReversal, IsSignFlip, WeightedStratumGapSum, SignedPooledGap, AllocationDistortion, DistortionType, PolicyImplication, ConfoundersInStudy, IsParadoxExplained
data.UserAction: Expand strata panel to see per-stratum breakdown; navigate to Strata Detail.

data.Title: Strata Detail
data.Purpose: Show every (stratum, treatment) cell for a study: StratumSuccessRate, AllocationBias, StratumGap, WeightedStratumGap — the leaf-level evidence for why the pooled conclusion is or isn't misleading.
data.KeyFields: StratumLabel, TreatmentLabel, StratumSuccessRate, StratumRateA, StratumRateB, StratumWinner, AllocationBias, AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, StratumGap, WeightedStratumGap, StratumFraction
data.UserAction: Hover a cell for the raw Successes/Cases count from CaseCells.

data.Title: Confounder Panel
data.Purpose: Show the stratifying variable's causal role for a study: VariableName, CausalRole, AffectsTreatmentAssignment, AffectsOutcome, IsConfounder, MechanismNote.
data.KeyFields: VariableName, CausalRole, AffectsTreatmentAssignment, AffectsOutcome, IsConfounder, MechanismNote
data.UserAction: Read the mechanism note; understand why IsParadoxExplained is TRUE or FALSE for this study.

data.Title: Distortion Plane
data.Purpose: Plot all studies on a 2D plane: AllocationDistortion (x-axis) × ReversalIntensity (y-axis), colored by DistortionType. Shows the geometric taxonomy at a glance and reveals how the four types partition the plane.
data.KeyFields: Title (from Studies), AllocationDistortion, ReversalIntensity, IsSignFlip, DistortionType, PolicyImplication, ParadoxStrength
data.UserAction: Click a data point to navigate to that study's Study Detail screen.

data.Title: Model Summary
data.Purpose: Show the instrument's self-portrait: how many studies, how many of each DistortionType, average AllocationDistortion, total paradox strength — the epistemic coverage of the full model instance.
data.KeyFields: StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeACount, TypeBCount, TypeCCount, TypeDCount, AvgAllocationDistortion, TotalParadoxStrength
data.UserAction: Navigate to Study List or Distortion Plane from this summary.

data.Title: Loop History
data.Purpose: Show the full build history: all 31 loops with their status, new concept, domain question, and mock data note. Lets a developer understand why the model is shaped the way it is — the DAG is the story.
data.KeyFields: LoopId, Title, Status, NewConcept, DomainQuestion, MockDataNote, NextSuggestion
data.UserAction: Filter by Status (complete/planned); click a loop to expand its full detail.

data.Title: Methodology
data.Purpose: Show the ten first-principles methodological axioms that shaped this model — from CMCC as the floor, through DAG-first decomposition, to the four-type taxonomy. Enables a developer to apply the same process to a new domain.
data.UserAction: Click IllustratedByLoop to jump to that loop in the Loop History screen.

data.Title: Conclusions
data.Purpose: Show all witnessed and planned conclusions, grouped by category (domain / instrument / methodology / open-question). The read-out of what this model has actually established.
data.KeyFields: Category, Status, Title, Evidence, WitnessedInLoop, TargetLoop
data.UserAction: Filter by Status (witnessed/planned/open); click WitnessedInLoop to navigate to Loop History.

UIComponents.Description: Table: UIComponents — the reusable visual building blocks used across UIScreens. Each row defines one component: what it renders, which entity it reads, and what props it requires. A developer can implement the full UI by composing these components onto the screens defined in UIScreens.

schema.name: ComponentId
schema.Description: Unique slug (e.g. comp-01-distortion-badge).

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label, mirrors ComponentId.

schema.name: ComponentName
schema.Description: Code-level component name (PascalCase, e.g. DistortionBadge).

schema.name: UsedOnScreens
schema.Description: Comma-separated ScreenIds where this component appears.

schema.name: PrimaryEntity
schema.Description: The rulebook entity this component reads (the vw_* view).

schema.Description: What this component renders and what decisions a developer must make to implement it.

schema.name: RequiredFields
schema.Description: Comma-separated rulebook fields this component must receive as props.

schema.name: VisualSpec
schema.Description: Plain-English description of the visual output: shape, color coding, interactivity.

data.Description: A colored pill/chip that shows DistortionType (A/B/C/D) and PolicyImplication for one study. Color-coded: A=red (stratify-immediately), B=orange (investigate-confounder), C=yellow (check-allocation-bias), D=green (pooled-analysis-trustworthy).
data.VisualSpec: Pill shape, 2–3 chars for the type letter, tooltip on hover showing PolicyImplication text. Consistent color across all screens.

data.Description: A side-by-side horizontal bar chart comparing PooledRateA vs PooledRateB (or StratumRateA vs StratumRateB for strata). The bar for the loser under equal-weight allocation is visually distinguished from the bar for the pooled loser.
data.RequiredFields: PooledRateA, PooledRateB, PooledWinner, WeightedStratumGapSum, SignedPooledGap, IsSignFlip
data.VisualSpec: Two horizontal bars, labeled A and B. Arrow or bracket showing the gap. IsSignFlip=TRUE triggers a 'reversal' annotation. Width proportional to rate (0–1 scale).

data.Description: A grid of (Stratum × Treatment) cells where cell color encodes AllocationBias magnitude and sign. Positive bias (A over-allocated here) in one color; negative (B over-allocated) in another. Cell text shows AllocationFractionA and AllocationFractionB.
data.RequiredFields: StratumLabel, TreatmentLabel, AllocationBias, AllocationFractionA, AllocationFractionB, StratumSuccessRate
data.VisualSpec: Matrix layout: rows=strata, cols=treatments. Diverging color scale centered at 0. Tooltip shows raw Cases and Successes from CaseCells.

data.Description: A 2D scatter plot with AllocationDistortion on the x-axis and ReversalIntensity on the y-axis. Each point is one study, colored by DistortionType. Quadrant lines at AllocationDistortion=0.01 (threshold) and ReversalIntensity=1.0 (unanimity) divide the plane into the four types.
data.VisualSpec: SVG or canvas scatter. Labeled quadrant regions. Clickable points. Legend showing type → color → policy action.

data.Description: A waterfall chart showing how each stratum's WeightedStratumGap contributes to WeightedStratumGapSum, then how the allocation re-weights them to produce SignedPooledGap. The visual makes the mechanism equation explicit: pooled = Σ(weight × rate).
data.RequiredFields: StratumLabel, StratumGap, StratumFraction, WeightedStratumGap, WeightedStratumGapSum, SignedPooledGap
data.VisualSpec: Waterfall/stacked bar: each stratum's WeightedStratumGap as a segment; running sum shown; final total vs actual SignedPooledGap shown side-by-side. IsSignFlip highlights the sign difference.

data.Description: A summary scorecard showing all ModelSummary fields as labeled metric tiles: StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, TypeA/B/C/D counts, AvgAllocationDistortion, TotalParadoxStrength.
data.RequiredFields: StudyCount, ReversalCount, ExplainedCount, ZeroStrengthCount, TotalParadoxStrength, AvgParadoxStrength
data.VisualSpec: Grid of metric tiles. Each tile: large number, small label. Color: ReversalCount in orange/red; ExplainedCount in green; TypeD in grey. Single row layout on desktop, 2-column on mobile.

data.Description: A card showing one StratumVariable row: VariableName, CausalRole badge, AffectsTreatmentAssignment checkbox, AffectsOutcome checkbox, IsConfounder result, and MechanismNote prose block.
data.RequiredFields: VariableName, CausalRole, AffectsTreatmentAssignment, AffectsOutcome, IsConfounder, MechanismNote
data.VisualSpec: Card with header=VariableName, CausalRole as a badge (confounder=red, mediator=orange, contested=yellow, unknown=grey), two boolean indicators, and MechanismNote as body text.

data.Description: A vertical timeline of all Loops rows, grouped by Status. Each entry shows LoopId, Title, Status indicator, NewConcept chip, and an expandable section with DomainQuestion, MockDataNote, and NextSuggestion.
data.RequiredFields: LoopId, Title, Status, NewConcept, DomainQuestion, MockDataNote, NextSuggestion
data.VisualSpec: Vertical list. Status=complete: solid circle; in-progress: half-filled; planned: open circle. Expandable accordion per entry. Color: complete=green, in-progress=blue, planned=grey.

data.Description: A prominent banner at the top of the Study Detail screen showing PolicyImplication as a clear action statement, color-coded by DistortionType. Includes a one-sentence explanation of why this action follows from the geometry.
data.VisualSpec: Full-width colored banner. Large icon (e.g. warning triangle for A, magnifying glass for B, scale for C, checkmark for D). Bold action text. Secondary line explaining the geometric basis.

data.Description: A filterable list of Conclusions rows grouped by Category and Status. Each entry shows the Title, Status badge, Evidence summary, and a link to the WitnessedInLoop or TargetLoop.
data.RequiredFields: Category, Status, Title, Evidence, WitnessedInLoop, TargetLoop
data.VisualSpec: Grouped list with category headers. Status badge: witnessed=green, planned=blue, open=orange. Evidence shown as collapsed text (expand on click). Loop links navigate to LoopTimeline.

InstrumentSpec.Description: Table: InstrumentSpec — the complete specification of the Simpson's Paradox classification instrument. One row per component: required input fields, derived coordinates, classification outputs, and adapter requirements. Every row is machine-verifiable against the 16 hydrated studies. This table is the distillation of loops 1–25 into a reusable tool.

schema.name: SpecId
schema.Description: Unique identifier for this instrument component.

schema.name: Name
schema.Description: Display label.

schema.name: ComponentType
schema.Description: input | derived | classification | adapter-requirement

schema.name: FieldName
schema.Description: The field or variable name in the DAG.

schema.name: DataType
schema.Description: integer | decimal | boolean | string | categorical

schema.name: SourceTable
schema.Description: The DAG table where this field is computed or stored.

schema.name: Formula
schema.Description: The ERB formula or derivation rule for this component.

schema.name: NaturalLanguage
schema.Description: Plain-English statement of what this component represents or requires.

schema.name: AdapterNote
schema.Description: What a researcher must supply or verify to satisfy this component when hydrating a new study.

data.NaturalLanguage: A unique identifier for the study being classified (e.g. 'kidney-1986').
data.AdapterNote: Assign a slug that is stable and unique. All rows for a study share this value.

data.NaturalLanguage: The label of the subgroup (stratum) for this cell — the variable being stratified on (e.g. 'large', 'high-risk', 'male').
data.AdapterNote: Must be consistent across the A and B cells within the same stratum. At least 2 strata required.

data.NaturalLanguage: Which treatment (exposure, group, class) this cell belongs to — 'A' or 'B'.
data.AdapterNote: Exactly two treatment labels per study. Encode the group you want to investigate as 'A'; comparator as 'B'.

data.NaturalLanguage: Count of successful outcomes (survived, passed, admitted, etc.) in this cell.
data.AdapterNote: Must be a non-negative integer ≤ Cases. Define 'success' consistently across all cells in the study.

data.NaturalLanguage: Total number of observations (trials, patients, applicants, etc.) in this cell.
data.AdapterNote: Must be a positive integer. Every (Study, Stratum, Treatment) combination must have exactly one CaseCell row.

data.NaturalLanguage: The success rate for this specific treatment within this specific stratum.
data.AdapterNote: Computed automatically. Range [0,1].

data.NaturalLanguage: The overall (pooled, unstratified) success rate for treatment A across all strata.
data.AdapterNote: Computed from SUMIFS across CaseCells. This is the rate a naive observer would report.

data.NaturalLanguage: The overall (pooled, unstratified) success rate for treatment B across all strata.
data.AdapterNote: Computed from SUMIFS across CaseCells.

data.NaturalLanguage: The signed difference in pooled rates (A minus B). Positive means A appears better pooled; negative means B appears better.
data.AdapterNote: The naive result — what the unadjusted analysis reports.

data.NaturalLanguage: The fraction of all treatment-A cases that fall in this stratum — treatment A's allocation weight.
data.AdapterNote: Sums to 1.0 across strata within a study.

data.NaturalLanguage: The signed per-stratum rate difference (A minus B). The true causal signal for this stratum, free of allocation effects.
data.AdapterNote: Computed from within-stratum aggregation of CaseCells.

data.NaturalLanguage: The stratum gap weighted by the average of A's and B's allocation fractions — the equal-weight contribution of this stratum.
data.AdapterNote: Summing across strata yields WeightedStratumGapSum.

data.NaturalLanguage: The equal-allocation benchmark: what SignedPooledGap would be if each treatment received the same case-mix. This is the allocation-free signal.
data.AdapterNote: Compare to SignedPooledGap to detect distortion.

data.NaturalLanguage: TRUE when the pooled winner (SignedPooledGap) points in the opposite direction from the equal-weight per-stratum winner (WeightedStratumGapSum). This is the sign of a reversal.
data.AdapterNote: The primary reversal detector.

data.NaturalLanguage: The absolute magnitude of the allocation distortion — how much the observed pooled gap differs from the equal-weight benchmark.
data.AdapterNote: Zero means no allocation confounding. > 0.01 is the classification threshold for types C and D.

data.NaturalLanguage: The four-type classification: A=full reversal (A wins all strata, B wins pooled); B=partial reversal (split strata, pooled contradicts weighted); C=distortion without reversal (same winner, but margin compressed or inflated by ≥1pp); D=trustworthy pooled (allocation-free or near-free).
data.AdapterNote: Output. Read-only for the researcher.

data.NaturalLanguage: The recommended analytic action given the distortion type: A→stratify immediately (pooled is misleading); B→investigate the confounder structure; C→report allocation bias alongside pooled result; D→pooled analysis is trustworthy.
data.AdapterNote: Output. This is the instrument's primary policy-facing deliverable.

data.NaturalLanguage: One Strata row per (study, stratum) combination, with a plain-English Description of what this subgroup represents and why it matters.
data.AdapterNote: Required for interpretability. The DAG computes rates without it, but the explanation of the paradox requires this context.

data.NaturalLanguage: One StratumVariables row per study naming the confounding variable, its CausalRole (confounder/mediator/contested), and a MechanismNote explaining how the allocation imbalance arises.
data.AdapterNote: CausalRole must be one of: confounder, mediator, contested, unknown. IsConfounder is derived (confounder AND both affect-flags true).

data.NaturalLanguage: Four StratumSummaries seed rows per study (one per stratum × treatment combination) — only StratumSummaryId, Study, StratumLabel, TreatmentLabel required; all computed fields are populated by the DAG.
data.AdapterNote: Minimum hydration contract: provide the four raw-key fields. The re-hydrate step fills in all derived values from the views.
